nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and estuaries of Queensland and, more recently, north-western Australia; typically encountered over algae-covered or muddy bottoms at depths of about 2–10 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Reef-associated, non-migratory; often found in turbid coastal waters and estuaries, commonly observed in pairs in the wild.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small benthic invertebrates in the wild, using its long, narrow snout to probe crevices. In captivity, generally needs a varied meaty diet — mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, frozen cockle and other finely chopped seafood, offered several small meals per day. New arrivals are often shy, deliberate feeders; live foods (live brine shrimp, blackworms) are commonly required to start them eating. Commonly considered effective at controlling Aiptasia (glass anemones).

summary: Australian endemic butterflyfish closely related to the Copperband, distinguished by a black-banded silver body and rusty hindquarters. Generally considered an advanced aquarium fish due to feeding challenges, but is commonly reported to adapt to captivity more readily than its Copperband relative.
careNotes: Endemic to Queensland and north-western Australian coastal reefs and estuaries, where it tolerates turbid water and silty/muddy substrates more readily than most butterflyfishes. Best housed in a mature, well-established reef or FOWLR aquarium (commonly advised at six months or older) alongside peaceful tankmates that won't out-compete it at feeding time. Generally advised to keep singly in aquaria — although wild fish are commonly seen in pairs, conspecifics and similar Chelmon species are commonly quarrelsome in captivity. Reef compatibility is rated 'with caution': has been reported to pick at feather dusters, tube worms, anemones, and occasionally ornamental invertebrates, while frequently consuming Aiptasia. Feeding the new arrival is the chief husbandry hurdle; many specimens refuse prepared foods initially and need live brine or blackworms before transitioning onto frozen mysis and chopped seafood. Tank-size sources spread widely — LiveAquaria advises 125 US gal (~475 L), reefapp.net recommends ~1000 L; 500 L was chosen as a cautious mid-large pick reflecting the species' need for swimming space, a long footprint, and undisturbed feeding territory. Temperature ranges across sources span LiveAquaria's 20–25 °C and FishBase's wild range of ~25–28.8 °C (mean 27.4 °C); the recorded 22–27 °C averages across both. Reported aquarium lifespan is not well-documented but is commonly estimated at ~5–10 years with excellent husbandry, by analogy with the closely related Copperband.
breedingNotes: Forms pair bonds in the wild and is oviparous, but has not been reliably bred in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes), max length (20.5 cm TL), wild temperature range (~25–28.8 °C, mean 27.4 °C), depth (2–10 m), Queensland/NW Australia endemism, and pair-forming behaviour in the wild.

additional.name: Reefapp.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium tank size guidance — recommends ~1000 L, materially higher than LiveAquaria's 125 US gal. Spread informed the cautious-large 500 L pick.
